1: wayyitˀonenu (וַיִּתְאֹנְנוּ) — a rare verb from the root ˀanan, appearing only here. The form is reflexive-intensive (hitpaˁel): they brought complaint against themselves and against God. The LXX renders it egoggyzan (grumbled), the same word Paul uses for the wilderness generation in 1 Corinthians 10:10. The fire at Taberah is YHWH's immediate judgment — no mediating cause, just direct divine response to the first sound of complaint.
2: wayyitpallel (וַיִּתְפַּלֵּל) — Moses prayed (hitpaˁel of palal = to intervene, judge). Moses' intercession stops the fire. The pattern repeats throughout Numbers: Israel sins → YHWH judges → Moses intercedes → judgment stops. Moses functions as the structural type of the one mediator (1 Timothy 2:5) who stands in the gap between a holy God and a sinning people.
3: Tabˁerah (תַּבְעֵרָה) — from baˁar (to burn, consume). The place receives its name from the event. The wilderness journey is narrated through its place names: each toponym encodes a theological moment. The landscape of Israel's failure becomes a lasting geography of warning.
4: asafsuf (אַסַפְסֻף) — hapax; from asaph (to gather). The 'mixed multitude' (cf. Exodus 12:38, ˁerev rav) who joined Israel from Egypt. Their craving (wayyitˀavvu taˀavah, וַיִּתְאַוּוּ תַּאֲוָה) is a figura etymologica: 'they craved a craving' — an intensified desire named by the root itself. The place Kibroth-hattaˀavah (Numbers 11:34) preserves the vocabulary: taˀavah = craving, desire, lust.
5: The list of Egyptian foods mourned — fish, cucumbers, melons, leeks, onions, garlic — is a memory of Egypt as provision without cost: ḥinnam (חִנָּם, free of charge). The irony is sharp: Egyptian labor was as far from free as any servitude could be. The memory of the slave diet has been filtered through craving into a fantasy of abundance. Craving distorts memory.
6: naphshenu yˁveshah (נַפְשֵׁנוּ יְבֵשָׁה) — 'our nefesh is parched/withered.' Nefesh here carries its primary physiological meaning (throat, gullet, the organ of desire and hunger) — their very swallowing apparatus has dried up. The complaint is that manna satisfies nothing: ein kol (אֵין כֹּל, there is nothing at all) — total dissatisfaction. The manna provided fully; the dissatisfaction was in the nefesh, not in the provision.
7: mann hu (מָן הוּא) — 'what is it?' The popular etymology of manna. The description: like zeraˁ gad (coriander seed, זֶרַע גַּד) and its color like keˁen haˀbdolaḥ (bdellium resin, בְּדֹלַח — also mentioned in Genesis 2:12 in the description of Eden's materials). The manna has an Edenic quality — a provision from the garden-state of the world.
8: The people shoṭetu (שָׁטְטוּ, traveled around gathering it) and ground it in mills or beat it in mortars. The description of manna preparation emphasizes the labor involved in receiving the gift. God's provision still requires human participation. The manna was not ready-cooked; it was material requiring transformation. Jesus' self-identification as the true bread from heaven (John 6:31-35) interprets Numbers 11:7-8.
9: The manna fell with the dew: biˀredet hatal (בְּרֶדֶת הַטָּל) — 'in the falling of the dew.' Dew in the OT is a symbol of divine blessing descending quietly overnight (Deuteronomy 32:2; Psalms 133:3; Proverbs 19:12). The manna arrives exactly as blessing arrives: silently, nightly, without human observation or effort. The morning reveals what God has done in the dark.
10: bˁozen YHWH (בְּאָזְנֵי יְהוָה) — 'in the ears of the LORD' (Numbers 11:1). YHWH's hearing of the complaint is not metaphorical; the text is anthropomorphic and deliberate. The cry reaches the divine ear and kindles the divine anger (wayˀiḥar ˀap-YHWH). Moses' distress at being caught between YHWH's anger and the people's weeping is the condition of the intercessor.
11: Moses uses the language of raˁah (רָעָה, evil/trouble): 'Why have you brought evil/trouble on your servant?' The intercessor is allowed frank speech — even accusatory speech — toward God. The same vocabulary of divine burden-shifting appears in the Psalms of lament. Moses' complaint in Numbers 11:11-15 is a model of honest prayer that does not mask difficulty with pious language.
12: heˀerothi (הֶאֱרִיתִי) — 'did I conceive them?' From harah (to conceive, be pregnant). Moses refuses the nursing-father role: kˀˀomen (כָּאֹמֵן, as a guardian/nurse, sometimes 'foster-father') — the same root gives emunah (faithfulness/trust) and ˀamen. The paradox: the root for nursing/trustworthy is also the root for the ultimate theological affirmation. Moses denies being Israel's ˀomen; Christ is Israel's ˀomen in the deepest sense (Hebrews 3:2: pistos, faithful).
13: mayyin asher-ˀeten (מֵאַיִן) — 'where am I to get?' The impossible demand: meat for six hundred thousand. Moses' lament exposes the mathematical absurdity of the people's demand. The provision that follows will be miraculous precisely because human calculation shows it impossible. God's generosity routinely outstrips human mathematics.
14: loˀ ˀukal ˀanochi levadi (לֹא אוּכַל אָנֹכִי לְבַדִּי) — 'I am not able — I alone.' The leadership crisis: one person cannot bear the whole people. The solution (70 elders sharing the Spirit) is the wilderness precursor of the apostolic commissioning, and ultimately of the Spirit poured out on all flesh (Joel 2:28). The inability of one person to bear God's people alone anticipates the communal structure of the Spirit.
15: Moses' extreme prayer: kill me right now (harog-ni naˀ harog) if I have found favor. The conditional favor-and-death request is dark; it mirrors Elijah's suicidal prayer (1 Kings 19:4). But both requests are met with provision and renewed commission rather than death. The servant who asks for death at the breaking point receives instead the power to continue.
16: ziqne Yisraˀel (זִקְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל, elders of Israel) — seventy men who serve as Israel's governing council. The number 70 recurs: 70 elders on Sinai (Exodus 24:1), 70 nations in the Table of Nations (Gen 10), 70 members of the later Sanhedrin. The number signifies totality and representativeness. What Moses cannot do alone, seventy representatives can share.
17: weˀatsalthi (וְאָצַלְתִּי) — from atsal (to set aside, set apart, withhold). YHWH will take from the ruaḥ (Spirit) that is on Moses and set a portion on the elders. The Spirit is not diminished in Moses by this distribution — unlike a finite physical substance, the Spirit's apportionment does not reduce the source. The Pentecost event (Acts 2) is the eschatological fulfillment: the Spirit poured out on all flesh, drawn from the inexhaustible source of the risen Christ.
18: hitqaddˀshu (הִתְקַדְּשׁוּ, consecrate yourselves) — qal reflexive (hitpaˁel) of qadash. The required preparation before meat: a day of consecration. The demand for meat will be met, but not before consecration. YHWH's giving of what is asked sometimes requires preparation that underscores the giver's holiness rather than merely satisfying the demand.
19: The emphatic negative list — not one day, not two, not five, not ten, not twenty days — builds toward the judgment-provision of Numbers 11:20. The repetition enacts the escalation of divine sarcasm: YHWH will give them exactly what they demanded, in excess. The rhetoric of the list makes the irony unavoidable before the punchline lands.
20: meˀastem ˀet-YHWH (מְאַסְתֶּם אֶת-יְהוָה) — 'you have rejected/despised the LORD who is among you.' The root maˀas = to reject, despise. Israel has not merely complained about food; they have rejected the one who provided it. The theological movement: dissatisfaction with provision → rejection of the provider. The manna was the daily visible presence of YHWH's care; refusing it was refusing him.
21: shesh-meˀot ˀelef ragli (שֵׁשׁ-מֵאוֹת אֶלֶף רַגְלִי) — six hundred thousand foot soldiers. Moses replicates the people's doubt in mathematical form: how can God feed this many? The number itself is the expression of impossibility. YHWH's response (Numbers 11:23: hayad YHWH tiqtsaˀr? — 'Is the hand of YHWH too short?') is the theological counter to all arithmetic of impossibility.
22: The rhetorical questions: would all the flocks and herds suffice? Would all the fish of the sea? Moses enumerates every natural category of provision and declares each insufficient. YHWH answers none of the specific questions; he simply asks whether his hand is short. The divine answer to 'how is this possible?' is not an explanation but a question about the questioner's theology of God's power.
23: hayad YHWH tiqtsaˀr? (הֲיַד יְהוָה תִּקְצָר?) — 'Is the hand of the LORD too short?' Yad (hand) = power, agency. qatsar (to be short, insufficient). The same phrase appears in Isaiah 59:1: 'The LORD's hand is not too short to save.' The wilderness question is answered definitively at the cross: the hand of YHWH that could not be shortened reaches all the way to death and back.
24: Moses gathers seventy elders and stations them around the tent. The spatial arrangement matters: the tent is the center of the Spirit's distribution. The elders are not gathered in Moses' home or in a place of their choosing; the Spirit is given from where YHWH is. Proximity to the divine presence is the condition of the Spirit's gift.
25: wayyitnabeˀu (וַיִּתְנַבְּאוּ) — they prophesied (hitpaˁel of navaˀ). The Spirit came on them and they prophesied — but they loˀ yasafu (לֹא יָסָפוּ, did not continue/add). The prophetic experience was one-time, not permanent. The elder council receives an initial Spirit-endowment for leadership, not the ongoing prophetic gift. The distinction between occasional and permanent spiritual empowerment runs through the OT.
26: Eldad and Medad had remained in the camp (wayyishshaˀaru bammaḥaneh). The Spirit did not wait for them to come to the tent; it found them where they were. The spatial theology of Numbers 11:26 is a wilderness precedent for the Spirit's freedom: it blows where it wills (John 3:8). Geographic or institutional non-attendance does not limit the Spirit's reach.
27: The report runs to Moses: mitnabeˀim bammaḥaneh — 'prophesying in the camp!' The alarm is institutional anxiety: prophetic activity was expected to occur at the tent under proper oversight. The wilderness situation of uncontrolled Spirit-distribution anticipates Paul's management of charismatic gifts in 1 Cor 12-14, and the Pentecostal explosion beyond Jerusalem in Acts 8-10.
28: Joshua's complaint: kˀleˀem (כְּלָאֵם, stop them / restrain them). Joshua is protecting Moses' unique prophetic office. His concern is institutional rather than theological: if everyone prophesies, what becomes of Moses' special status? Moses' response exposes the poverty of this thinking: real spiritual leadership wants others to exceed itself.
29: mi-yitten kol-ˁam YHWH nˀviˀim (מִי יִתֵּן כָּל-עַם יְהוָה נְבִיאִים) — 'Oh that all the LORD's people were prophets!' Moses' deepest wish: the democratization of the Spirit. The optative mi-yitten (who will give?) is the wishful idiom of desire. Joel 2:28-29 promises that this will happen: 'I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh... your sons and daughters will prophesy.' Acts 2:17 declares it fulfilled. The Mosaic wish is the Pentecost prophecy.
30: Moses and the elders returned to the camp. The Spirit's distribution complete, ordinary communal life resumes. The Spirit is not incompatible with ordinary leadership and daily life; the prophetic eruption at the tent was an event, not a permanent disruption of order.
31: ruaḥ nasaˁ meˀet YHWH (רוּחַ נָסַע מֵאֵת יְהוָה) — 'a wind/spirit went out from YHWH.' The same word ruaḥ (spirit/wind) used for the Spirit on the elders now describes the wind bringing the quail. The word play is probably intentional: YHWH sends both kinds of ruaḥ — the Spirit of prophecy and the wind of provision. Both are from him; both reveal his power.
32: The people gather quail obsessively: all day, all night, all the next day. The one who gathered least took ten homers (a homer ≈ 220 liters). The excess enacts the judgment-gift: YHWH will give them what they demand until they are sick of it (Numbers 11:20). Ungoverned craving receives exactly what it wants, in exactly the form it should have feared.
33: habbaser ˁod ben shinneyhem (הַבָּשָׂר עוֹד בֵּין שִׁנֵּיהֶם) — 'the meat was still between their teeth.' The judgment fell before the first meal was finished. The speed of divine response to presumptuous consumption underscores that YHWH's patience had already been exhausted before the quail arrived. The anger was latent in the giving; the gift was already a judgment.
34: Qivroth-hattaˀavah (קִבְרוֹת הַתַּאֲוָה) — 'Graves of the Craving/Desire.' The place where the people buried those struck by plague. The etymology becomes the epitaph: where craving ruled, burial follows. The naming of places in Israel's wilderness journey serves as a theological mnemonic — the landscape teaches the theology of sin and consequence.
35: The itinerary resumes: from Kibroth-hattaavah to Hazeroth. The wilderness journey continues immediately after burial. Israel does not stop to process; the march goes on. The relentlessness of the wilderness journey is itself part of the formation: there is no settling down in a place of failure, no permanent dwelling in the graves of desire.
